coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Jimei
Zhang, Heng
Peng, Fangfang
Yang, Xiaoqing
Li, Zi-Long
Sun, Linhao
Jiang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine via one-step under mild condition and used as a sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for the activity determination of N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase (NAG). In this assay, p-nitrophenol was in situ produced from p-nitrophenyl-β-D-N-acetyl-glucosaminide, which was exclusively hydrolyzed by NAG. The UV absorption peak of p-nitrophenol (maximum at 400 nm) overlapped the excitation peak of CDs with maximum wavelength at 415 nm, which caused the fluorescence decline of CDs based on inner filter effect. The activity of NAG was determined by the fluorescence changes. The assay is highly sensitive to NAG with a low detection limit of 0.75 U L−1 (K = 3) and showed a good linear relationship in the range from 1 to 45 U L−1. This CDs nanoprobe was successfully applied for the determination of NAG activity in human serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Inner filter effect
N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase
Human serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism based on inner filter effect
Application of NAG activity detection in human samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of CDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (C) XPS spectrum of CDs. (D) (E) (F) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs excited by different wavelengths. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs (Inset: Images of CDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV irradiation (right)).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different substance (1 mM). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of Nag-PNP and PNP (solid line), and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs (dotted line). (C) Fluorescence decay curve of CDs in the absence (solid line) and presence (dotted line) of PNP.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Nag-PNP (1 mM) with different activity of NAG. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (0.05 mg mL−1) with different activities of NAG. (C) The plots of I0–I versus the activities of NAG between1-45 U L−1.

(A) (B) Fluorescence responses of CDs (0.05 mg mL−1) at 520 nm to different interferences (2 mM or 50 U L−1) in the absence and presence of NAG (50 U L−1) (I0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 520 nm when only CDs and Nag-PNP (1 mM) were added, recorded as the blank group.).
The mechanism of CDs nanoprobe for NAG activity detection.
Detection for NAG activity in human serum samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Serum sample from the same person.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The found results were the mean of three measurements±standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovery (%) = [(Found value-blank value)/added value] × 100%, the blank value was sample 1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection for NAG activity in human urine samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The found results were the mean of three measurements±standard deviation (SD).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, 430070, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Affiliated Hospital of Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Huazhong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Affiliated Hospital of Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine via one-step under mild condition and used as a sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for the activity determination of N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase (NAG). In this assay, p-nitrophenol was in situ produced from p-nitrophenyl-β-D-N-acetyl-glucosaminide, which was exclusively hydrolyzed by NAG. The UV absorption peak of p-nitrophenol (maximum at 400 nm) overlapped the excitation peak of CDs with maximum wavelength at 415 nm, which caused the fluorescence decline of CDs based on inner filter effect. The activity of NAG was determined by the fluorescence changes. The assay is highly sensitive to NAG with a low detection limit of 0.75 U L−1 (K = 3) and showed a good linear relationship in the range from 1 to 45 U L−1. This CDs nanoprobe was successfully applied for the determination of NAG activity in human serum and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetic nephropathy, a common complication occurred in near 40% of diabetic patients, is a major reason of chronic kidney disease and prone to resulting in kidney failure. Early diagnosis of diabetic nephropathy is necessary for diabetic patient to get timely and proper treatment. Because the early indicator albuminuria is not completely accurate for clinical diagnosis, a few new biomarkers based on proteomics have been studied as alternatives or additional indicators [1,2]. N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase (NAG) is a hydrolytic enzyme mainly existing in lysosomes of proximal tubules. It usually participates in mucopolysaccharide and glycoprotein metabolism of tubular basement membrane. The content of NAG increases with the aggravation of renal tubular cell damage and hyperglycemia [3]. However, NAG cannot penetrate the glomerular filtration barrier. Consequently, urinary NAG is commonly used as a biomarker for diabetic nephropathy [4–6]. The traditional colorimetric method was commonly used for the detection of NAG. However, it generally suffered background interference and low sensitivity. Till now, only a few new detection methods was reported, for example, electrochemiluminescence immunosensor [7,8] and enzyme-activated fluorescent method [9]. Simple and highly sensitive strategies are still urgent to be developed.

Fluorescent carbon-based nanoprobe has been widely used for rapid and sensitive detection of biomolecules [10–12]. Our group has developed polymer dots-based nanoprobe for the determination of the activity of α-glucosidase, α-l-fucosidase, alkaline phosphatase and horseradish peroxidase [13,14]. Among the carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have received great attention because of their low cost, chemical inertness, excellent optical property, low cytotoxicity and so on. However, most CDs are prepared through hydrothermal method [15].

In this work, CDs with a size of 2–5 nm was prepared under mild conditions and used a fluorescent nanoprobe for the activity determination of NAG. CDs were prepared from p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine and glowed green fluorescence. The maximum excitation wavelength of CDs was 415 nm and the maximum emission wavelengths was 520 nm. In the detection system, p-nitrophenyl-β-D-N-acetyl- glucosaminide was used as a substrate, which was exclusively hydrolyzed by NAG. The content of hydrolyzed product p-nitrophenol was increased with the quantity of NAG. Since the UV absorption peak of p-nitrophenol (maximum at 400 nm) overlapped the excitation peak of CDs with maximum wavelength at 415 nm, the green fluorescence of CDs was decayed based on inner filter effect (IFE) (Scheme 1 ). The activity of NAG was then determined by the fluorescence changes. To verify the practicability of this CDs nanoprobe, it was further applied for the determination of NAG activity in both human serum and urine samples.

N-Acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase (NAG) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. p-Nitrophenyl-β-D-N-acetyl-glucosaminide (Nag-PNP), p-nitrophenol (PNP) and p-aminophenol (PAP) were obtained from J&K Scientific. Ethylenediamine, hydrochloric acid, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaOH were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Serine (Ser), Alanine (Ala), glutamic acid (Glu), asparagic acid (Asp), leucine (Leu), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), lysine (Lys), tyrosine (Tyr), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and glutathione (GSH) were bought from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP), lipase and α-glucosidase were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure water was collected from Millipore Milli-Q Academic water purification system. The human serum samples and urine samples were collected from the Affiliated Hospital of Huazhong Agricultural University.

Field mission Transmission electron microscopy (FTEM) was performed on Talos F200X transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded on a UV-2500 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra were taken on Bruker Tensor 27 FT-IR (BURKER Company, Ettlingen, Germany). Fluorescence spectrum was obtained by operating on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with xenon lamp. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on Thermo Escalab 250XI (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). The enzymatic reaction was performed in a water bath thermostatic oscillator.

The synthesis of CDs was optimized inspired by the reported method [16] (Details in supporting information, Fig. S1). p-Aminophenol solution (1 M, 5 mL) and ethylenediamine solution (1 M, 5 mL) were prepared with phosphate buffer solution (pH = 5.0, 10 mM) separately. Then, the above two solutions were mixed together and diluted to 20 mL with phosphate buffer solution and stirred for 5 h at 50 °C. After that, the solvent was evaporated under vacuo, and the product was obtained as a black solid powder by column chromatography purification with ethyl acetate.

Prior to quantitative detection of NAG activity, the optimal conditions for the catalytic hydrolysis of Nag-PNP by NAG were firstly established (Details in supporting information, Fig. S2). NAG solutions with various activities (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 17.5, 20, 22.5, 25, 30, 35, 37.5, 40, 42.5, 45, 47.5, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 200, 500 U L−1) were prepared with phosphate buffer solution (pH = 4.5, 10 mM). Then, NAG solutions with different activities and Nag-PNP solutions (1 mM, 500 μL) were homogeneous mixed and shaken up in the oscillator. After incubation at 37 °C for 40 min, CDs solutions (0.05 mg mL−1, 500 μL) were added into the mixtures. The fluorescence intensities of the mixtures were then measured, respectively.

The selectivity experiments were performed. Firstly, 27 groups of mixtures of CDs solutions (0.05 mg mL−1, 500 μL) and Nag-PNP solutions (1 mM, 500 μL) were prepared. A series of interferences were respectively added to the mixtures, including amino acids as Ala, Asp, Cys, Glu, His, Leu, Lys, Ser, Tyr (2 mM, 50 μL), metal ions as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ (2 mM, 50 μL), enzymes and other substances as HRP, lipase, α-glucosidase, α-l-fucosidase (AFU), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), glucose oxidase (GOX), tyrosinase (TYR), β-glucosidase (β-Glu), β-galactosidase (β-Gal) (50 U L−1, 50 μL) and BSA, GSH (20 mg L−1, 50 μL). Another set of identical mixtures was then prepared and NAG (50 U L−1, 50 μL) was added to each one. Then, they were reacted for 40 min at 37 °C. Fluorescence intensities of the mixtures with and without NAG were respectively measured.

Four sets of identical human serum sample solutions were taken and different activities of NAG (0, 10 U L−1, 30 U L−1, 50 U L−1) were added thereto. After that, CDs solutions (0.05 mg mL−1, 500 μL), Nag-PNP solutions (1 mM, 500 μL) and the four human serum samples were respectively mixed and reacted at 37 °C for 40 min and the fluorescence intensities were recorded. The NAG activities of five different human urine samples were respectively measured by the similar procedure.

The CDs were synthesized by a simple heating and stirring reaction of p-aminophenol and ethylenediamine. The raw materials are safe, cheap and readily available. The synthesis was simple and efficient without any harsh conditions. The obtained CDs showed strong fluorescence. Then, the prepared CDs were characterized by FTEM, FT-IR, XPS, XRD and fluorescence spectra experiments.

It can be observed from the HRTEM image (Fig. 1 A) that the shape of the CDs was spherical with a narrow diameter distribution of 2–5 nm. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment showed a particle size of 26.7 nm in average (Fig. S3), which was larger than the average particle size in the TEM image, because the result measured by DLS was the hydrated particle size [17]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1B) displays that CDs consist of associated hydroxyl (-OH) group (peak at 3439 cm−1) and carbonyl group (CO, peak at 1630 cm−1) [18]. The peaks at 1392 cm−1 and 1032 cm−1 was ascribed to C–O and C–O–C stretching vibrations, respectively [19]. Fig. 1C shows the full-scale XPS spectrum of CDs, where the peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.2 eV and 532.3 eV are ascribed to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution spectra of C 1s in Fig. 1D shows four distinct peaks at 284.0 eV, 284.7 eV, 285.6 eV and 286.5 eV, which represent CC, C–C, C–N and C–O, respectively [20]. As shown in Fig. 1E, the peak of N1s could be subdivided into three peaks (398.6, 399.3 and 400.2 eV), which were attributed to CN, N–H and C–N [21]. From the high-resolution spectra of O1s (Fig. 1F), the appearance of peaks at 532.3 eV and 533.0 eV further indicated the presence of CO and C–O on the surface of CDs [9]. The above characterization results illustrated that the new CDs were successfully synthesized by the reaction of ethylenediamine and p-aminophenol.

Different excitation wavelengths (360, 380, 390, 400, 405, 408, 410, 415, 420 nm) was screened to obtain the emission spectra. A set of broad peaks between 450 and 700 nm with a constant maximum peak of 520 nm were observed (Fig. 2 A). However, the fluorescence intensity of emission peak was strongest when CDs were excited at 415 nm. Fig. 2B exhibits that the maximum excitation and emission peak of CDs was individually located at 415 and 520 nm. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2B, the CDs solution showed strong green fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism based on inner filter effect
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that when both PNP and CDs were present, the fluorescence of CDs would be significantly attenuated or even quenched, while the presence of Nag-PNP had little effect on the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 3 A). As exhibited in Fig. 3B, the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the Nag-PNP represented by the solid line a showed a maximum absorption peak at 300 nm. The solid line b was the ultraviolet absorption peak of PNP produced by the hydrolysis of Nag-PNP by NAG, and the maximum absorption peak appeared around 400 nm. The dotted line c between 320 nm and 500 nm was the excitation spectrum of CDs, which had the highest fluorescence intensity at 415 nm. Meanwhile, there was a broad range coincidence between the excitation spectrum of CDs and the ultraviolet absorption peak of PNP, which triggered the IFE between CDs and PNP and brought the fluorescence quenching of CDs. The zeta potential of CDs and PNP were individually measured in deionized water to further verify the IFE quenching mechanism. The zeta potential of CDs was 3.67 mV, while PNP was −1.80 mV (Fig. S4)., The electrostatic interaction between CDs and PNP could not be defined accurately since the two potentials were opposite. Thus, the fluorescence lifetime decay test of CDs was carried out in the absence and presence of PNP, respectively, and the fluorescence lifetime decay curves were consistent in both conditions as shown in Fig. 3C. Then, it was inferred that the fluorescence effect between CDs and PNP was constructed on the IFE quenching mechanism [13,20].

Before quantitative determination of NAG activity, the hydrolysis conditions of NAG were optimized at various pH values (4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5), temperature (25, 30, 35, 37, 40, 45 °C) and hydrolysis time (30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 min). It was found that the hydrolysis effect was maximized when the assay was performed at 37 °C and pH = 4.5 for 40 min (Fig. S2). The analytical time is less than that of the reported method [9] and acceptable.

Nag-PNP (Abs = 300 nm) was gradually hydrolyzed into PNP (Abs = 400 nm) with the addition of NAG, which could cause IFE effect with CDs. The fluorescence decay of CDs was observed as shown in the UV absorption spectra of Fig. 4 A. Therefore, enzyme activity of NAG was detected by the attenuation degree of CDs. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded after adding Nag-PNP and various concentrations of NAG from 0 to 500 U L−1 (Fig. 4B) under the optimal reaction conditions. As the NAG concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of CDs steadily decreased. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm exhibited a good linear relationship with the NAG activity within the range of 1.0–45 U L−1. A linear regression equation y = 90.89x+948.99 (R2 = 0.9944) was obtained (Fig. 4C). Based on the IUPAC definition (K = 3) [21], the detection limit was calculated to be 0.75 U L−1, which was lower than previous reports [9,22].

The result of the selectivity experiments is shown in Fig. 5 A and B. When only CDs, Nag-PNP and other interferences were added, the obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs could only occur when NAG was present (Fig. 5A). However, when NAG was added to all the mixtures, fluorescence quenching almost kept constant in each mixture (Fig. 5B). The above results indicated that these interferences (Ala, Asp, Cys, Glu, His, Leu, Lys, Ser, Tyr, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, HRP, lipase, α-glucosidase, BSA, GSH, AFU, ALP, GOX, TRY, β-glucosidase and β-galactosidase) have no effect on NAG detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NAG activity detection in human samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the practicability of the fluorescent nanoprobe, the activities of NAG in human serum and urine samples were tested respectively, and the experimental results were displayed in Table 1 and Table 2 . The serum samples recoveries of NAG activity ranged from 90.90% to 98.50% with relative standard deviation (RSD) between 1.85% and 3.19%. The urine samples RSD of NAG activity were between 1.64% and 4.80%. The measured activity of NAG in human serum and urine samples was in the normal range as reported [22,23]. The results indicated that the fluorescent nanoprobe had the potential for biological and medical experiments.

In summary, CDs with strong green fluorescence was prepared from cheap organic compounds under mild conditions. The CDs were demonstrated as an effective nanoprobe to construct detection system with Nag-PNP as substrate for the determination of NAG activity. The detecting assay showed high specificity and good linear relationship within the range of 1.0–45 U L−1. The method was successfully applied for the quantification of NAG activity in both healthy human serum and urine samples.

Dr. Jimei Ma and Prof. Hong Jiang constructed the idea and designed the whole assay. Dr. Jimei Ma provided the funding support and wrote the paper. Heng Zhang and Fangfang Peng operated all the detailed experiments. Xiaoqing Yang participated the assay of serum. Dr. Zi-Long Li was in charge of the characterization of nanomaterials. Dr. Linhao sun also provided the funding support and helped in improving the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (2662018JC011, 2662019PY023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.12.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nomenclature, symbols, units and their usage in spectrochemical analysis—II. data interpretation Analytical chemistry division
